input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete setup plan for .NET MAUI on Windows 11 (Android + Windows targets):

---

## 1. Install the .NET SDK (if not already installed)

```powershell
# Check if dotnet is installed
dotnet --list-sdks

# If not installed, use winget:
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9
```

## 2. Install the MAUI workload

```powershell
# See what workloads are available / installed
dotnet workload search maui
dotnet workload list

# Install MAUI (includes Android, Windows/WinUI)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify
dotnet workload list
```

## 3. Install Android SDK dependencies

The MAUI workload bundles Android SDK components, but you may need to accept licenses and confirm paths.

```powershell
# Find where the Android SDK was provisioned
$androidSdk = Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA "Microsoft\Android\sdk"
if (!(Test-Path $androidSdk)) {
    # Fallback: check the dotnet android default
    $androidSdk = Join-Path $env:USERPROFILE ".android\sdk"
}
Write-Host "Android SDK path: $androidSdk"

# List installed Android platforms & build-tools dynamically
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list 2>$null |
    Select-String "platforms;|build-tools;"

# Accept all licenses
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --licenses
```

If `cmdline-tools` isn't present (common when only the workload is installed), the MAUI build will auto-download what it needs on first build. You can also install standalone:

```powershell
winget install Google.AndroidCommandLineTools
```

## 4. Set up Android emulator (optional but recommended)

```powershell
$sdkMgr = "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat"

# Install emulator + a system image (discover latest dynamically)
& $sdkMgr "emulator" "platform-tools"

# List available system images
& $sdkMgr --list | Select-String "system-images;android-\d+;google_apis;x86_64"

# Install one (pick the highest API level from the list above)
& $sdkMgr "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64"

# Create an AVD
$avdMgr = "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat"
& $avdMgr create avd -n "Pixel_MAUI" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" --device "pixel_6"

# Launch it
& "$androidSdk\emulator\emulator.exe" -avd Pixel_MAUI
```

## 5. Enable Windows App SDK target (WinUI 3)

```powershell
# Enable Developer Mode (required for Windows target)
Start-Process "ms-settings:developers"
# → Toggle "Developer Mode" ON in the Settings window that opens

# The Windows App SDK is bundled with the MAUI workload.
# Confirm it's present:
dotnet workload list | Select-String "maui-windows"
```

## 6. Create and build a test project

```powershell
# Scaffold a new MAUI app
dotnet new maui -n HelloMaui
cd HelloMaui

# Discover available target frameworks in the project
Select-String 'TargetFrameworks' *.csproj

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net9.0-android

# Build for Windows
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Android emulator (emulator must be running)
dotnet run -f net9.0-android
```

## 7. (Optional) Install Visual Studio for full GUI/designer support

```powershell
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community

# Then add the MAUI workload via VS Installer, or:
# & "C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\Installer\setup.exe" modify `
#   --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.NetCrossPlat
```

---

**Key version-discovery tips:**
- `dotnet workload search` and `dotnet --list-sdks` always show current available/installed versions.
- `sdkmanager.bat --list` shows all installable Android SDK components with version numbers.
- The `TargetFrameworks` line in the `.csproj` tells you exactly which TFMs to pass to `-f`.

All commands are idempotent — safe to re-run if something fails partway through.
